reason: Bill establishes commission to audit Medicare/Social Security—classic precursor to entitlement reform. 'For other purposes' language + commission structure signals potential Trojan horse for benefit cuts, means-testing, or age/eligibility shifts buried in final recommendations.
vectors: Entitlement erosion: Medicare/Social Security framework audit may stage benefit reductions
Regulatory capture: Commission composition/charter could be stacked to favor deficit-hawk or insurance-industry recommendations
Fiscal bleed: 'Sustaining' framing obscures whether commission will recommend revenue increases vs. benefit cuts—asymmetric language suggests latter
Hidden riders: 'For other purposes' placeholder requires full text review for unrelated appropriations or carve-outs
title: To establish the Commission on Sustaining Medicare and Social Security, and for other purposes.

reason: Eliminates network adequacy requirements under ACA—classic regulatory capture vector disguised as patient choice. Forces insurers into open-access model that benefits high-volume/low-margin providers while dismantling negotiating leverage that kept premiums contained.
vectors: Regulatory carve-out: removes network standards, hollows enforcement mechanism
Market-access exploit: opens captive negotiation chains; benefits national/corporate providers over regional systems
Cost-shift mechanics: unbounded access inflates utilization; costs shift to premiums or cost-sharing
Insurance architecture sabotage: destabilizes risk pools by inverting adverse selection incentives
title: To amend the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act to provide that qualified health plans are not required to use a provider network.
